Subproblem 1: Given the following set of molecules (SMILES): Molecule A: ClC1=CC=C(C=C1)C=1N(C=CC1C)C, Molecule B: Oc1ccc(Oc2ccc(F)cc2)cc1 and Molecule C: O=C1CCCCc2ccccc21
Select the molecule containing a tertiary amine (use the SMARTS [NH0,nH0]). Consider the selected mol as "Mol-1".

Subproblem 2: Choose the SMILES from the following options (C1(CO1)CC(=C)Cl, C1(CC(Cl)=O)CC1, C1(CC1)OC(=C)Cl) that is equivalent to the following SMILES C1(CC1)CC(=O)Cl. Consider the selected mol as "Mol-2".

Subproblem 3: Given the molecules implicitly selected in Subproblem #1 (**Mol-1**) and Subproblem #2 (**Mol-2**), predict the major product (SMILES) formed after performing a reaction under the given conditions: Solvent: ClC1=CC=CC=C1, C1(=CC=CC=C1)C
Catalyst: [Zn] and Temperature: 25.0°C. If any essential reaction components or conditions are not specified, use reasonable standard choices based on the reaction type. Consider the selected mol as "Mol-3".

Subproblem 4: Given the molecules: Molecule A: C(=C/c1cn2nccc(N3CCOCC3)c2n1)\c1ccc2ccccc2n1, Molecule B: BrN1C(CCC1=O)=O and Molecule C: COCCn1ccc(-c2ccsc2)c1C(=O)C(=O)Nc1ccc(N2CCN(c3cc(C)cc(C)n3)CC2)cc1
Select the molecule that contains exactly 0 aromatic rings. Consider the selected mol as "Mol-4".

Subproblem 5: Using the reaction product obtained in Subproblem #3 (**Mol-3**) and the molecule selected in Subproblem #4 (**Mol-4**), predict the major product (SMILES) formed after performing a reaction under the given conditions: Solvent: C1CCOC1 and Temperature: -78.0°C. If any essential reaction components or conditions are not specified, use reasonable standard choices based on the reaction type. Consider the selected mol as "Mol-5".

Subproblem 6: Given the reaction product molecule obtained in Subproblem #5 (**Mol-5**), identify if this molecule contains the furan_ring substructure. Provide your answer as "Yes" or "No".

Subproblem 7: Based on your previous substructure matching result (Subproblem #6), select a molecule from the following set: Molecule J: C1(CC1)C1=NNC(=C1)N, Molecule K: CS(=O)(=O)c1cnc(-c2ccc(F)cc2F)nc1-c1cccc(C(F)(F)F)c1, that matches this criterion:
If your answer was "Yes" in Subproblem #6, select the molecule containing [CH3][S]=[O].
If your answer was "No," select the molecule **not containing** [CH3][S]=[O]. Consider the selected mol as "Mol-6".

Subproblem 8: Select the molecule from the set (**N#CSc1c(-c2ncon2)nn(-c2c(Cl)cc(C(F)(F)F)cc2Cl)c1N**, **N#Cc1nn(-c2c(Cl)cc(C(F)(F)F)cc2Cl)c(-n2cncn2)c1S(=O)(=O)C(F)(F)F**, **Cc1[nH]c(C(=O)Oc2c(F)c(F)c(F)c(F)c2F)cc1Br**) with the most implicit hydrogen atoms. If they have the same number of implicit hydrogens, break the tie by selecting the one with the highest molecular weight. Consider the selected mol as "Mol-7".

Subproblem 9: Take your answer for Mol-7 from the previous question. Does it have more rings than O=C([O-])CCON(Cc1ccccc1)Cc1ccccc1? If not, consider Mol-8 to be the molecule out of (**COc1ccc2c(c1)S(=O)(=O)C=C2Oc1ccc(Br)cc1**, **NC(=O)c1cccc(CCc2ccc(N3CC(=O)NS3(=O)=O)c(O)c2)c1**, **O=C(O)c1ccc(N2CCCS2(=O)=O)cc1N1CCCS1(=O)=O**) with 3rd minimum similarity with O=C([O-])CCON(Cc1ccccc1)Cc1ccccc1.
The similarity is defined with respect to the maximum common substructure of the two molecules (MCS Similarity):
MCS Similarity = # atoms maximum common substructure(a, b) / min (atoms in a, atoms in b)
If yes, Mol-8 is Mol-7.

Subproblem 13: Consider Mol-5 (from Subproblem #5) alongside the following list of candidate molecules Apply the criteria below to this entire set of candidates and select the single molecule that satisfies all criteria as Reactant 1.
Candidates: CC(=O)OC(=O)C, CC1(O)CCC1, COCc1c[nH]cn1, OC1C=C(I)CC1, Nc1ccc(N)cc1, CCOC(=O)C(N)=S, O=C1CC(O)CO1, O=c1cc(Br)cc[nH]1, CC(C)C(=O)CBr, CC(C)CS(=O)(=O)[O-]

Subproblem 14: Consider  **Mol-12, Mol-7, Mol-5, C=CCN/C(=N/C(=O)OCc1ccccc1)NC(=O)OCc1ccccc1** and select among them the molecule with the 2nd largest Wiener index, defined as:

W(G) = Σ d(i,j) for i < j

Where G is the molecular graph, and i and j are atom indices.
d(i,j) = shortest-path distance (in bonds) between atoms i and j.

The sum runs over all unordered atom pairs.

That is, the Wiener index is the sum of all pairwise topological distances.

Consider the selected mol as "Mol-13".

Subproblem 16: Take Mol-11, Mol-13, and Mol-14. First, order them by topological diameter. A molecule's topological diameter is equal to the largest eccentricity of any of its atoms. An atom’s eccentricity is the largest shortest path distance to any other atom. If there is a tie, order the tied elements by molecular weight. Descending order. Ignore Hydrogens. 

Provide a list with [[num_rotatable_bonds, heavy_atom_count, total_number_of_bonds, topological_diameter], [num_rotatable_bonds, heavy_atom_count, total_number_of_bonds, topological_diameter], [num_rotatable_bonds, heavy_atom_count, total_number_of_bonds, topological_diameter]] with the inner lists in that selected order as your output

Subproblem 16: Consider Mol-14 and Mol-13. Compute the Balaban J value of both molecules.
Round up to two decimal places. Return as a list with this format: [Mol-14_SMILES, Balaban_J_Mol_14, Mol-13_SMILES, Balaban_J_Mol_13]
Balaban J is given by the following definition. Consider the hydrogen-suppressed heavy-atom graph:
Build the topological distance matrix (D) on the undirected graph (G=(V,E)). Distances are shortest path lengths in edges; bond orders, charges, isotopes ignored.
For each atom i in V, compute its distance sum (S_i=\sum_{j\in V} D_{ij}).
Let (n=|V|), (m=|E|), and (c) be the number of connected components. Cyclomatic number (\mu=m-n+c).
Balaban index:
J(G)=\frac{m}{\mu+1}\sum_{(i,j)\in E}\frac{1}{\sqrt{S_i S_j}}.
When you are done, return your final answer in the format: solution = <answer to final subproblem>.

Balaban J is given by the following definition. Consider the hydrogen-suppressed heavy-atom graph:
Build the topological distance matrix (D) on the undirected graph (G=(V,E)). Distances are shortest path lengths in edges; bond orders, charges, isotopes ignored.

Let (n=|V|), (m=|E|), and (c) be the number of connected components. Cyclomatic number (\mu=m-n+c).
Balaban index:
J(G)=\frac{m}{\mu+1}\sum_{(i,j)\in E}\frac{1}{\sqrt{S_i S_j}}.

Balaban J is given by the following definition. Consider the hydrogen-suppressed heavy-atom graph:
Build the topological distance matrix (D) on the undirected graph (G=(V,E)). Distances are shortest path lengths in edges; bond orders, charges, isotopes ignored.
For each atom i in V, compute its distance sum (S_i=\sum_{j\in V} D_{ij}).
Let (n=|V|), (m=|E|), and (c) be the number of connected components. Cyclomatic number (\mu=m-n+c).
Balaban index:
J(G)=\frac{m}{\mu+1}\sum_{(i,j)\in E}\frac{1}{\sqrt{S_i S_j}}.

Balaban J is given by the following definition. Consider the hydrogen-suppressed heavy-atom graph:

Balaban J is given by the following definition. Consider the hydrogen-suppressed heavy-atom graph:
Build the topological distance matrix (D) on the undirected graph (G=(V,E)). Distances are shortest path lengths in edges; bond orders, charges, isotopes ignored.
For each atom i in V, compute its distance sum (S_i=\sum_{j\in V} D_{ij}).
Let (n=|V|), (m=|E|), and (c) be the number of connected components. Cyclomatic number (\mu=m-n+c).
Balaban index:
J(G)=\frac{m}{\mu+1}\sum_{(i,j)\in E}\frac{1}{\sqrt{S_i S_j}}.

Balaban J is given by the following definition. Consider the hydrogen-suppressed heavy-atom graph:
Build the topological distance matrix (D) on the undirected graph (G=(V,E)). Distances are shortest path lengths in edges; bond orders, charges, isotopes ignored.
For each atom i in V, compute its distance sum (S_i=\sum_{j\in V} D_{ij}).
Let (n=|V|), (m=|E|), and (c) be the number of connected components. Cyclomatic number (\mu=m-n+c).
Balaban index:
J(G)=\frac{m}{\mu+1}\sum_{(i,j)\in E}\frac{1}{\sqrt{S_i S_j}}.

Balaban J is given by the following definition. Consider the hydrogen-suppressed heavy-atom graph:
Build the topological distance matrix (D) on the undirected graph (G=(V,E)). Distances are shortest path lengths in edges; bond orders, charges, isotopes ignored.

Let (n=|V|), (m=|E|), and (c) be the number of connected components. Cyclomatic number (\mu=m-n+c).
Balaban index:
J(G)=\frac{m}{\mu+1}\sum_{(i,j)\in E}\frac{1}{\sqrt{S_i S_j}}.

Balaban J is given by the following definition. Consider the hydrogen-suppressed heavy-atom graph:
Build the topological distance matrix (D) on the undirected graph (G=(V,E)). Distances are shortest path lengths in edges; bond orders, charges, isotopes ignored.

Balaban J is given by the following definition. Consider the hydrogen-suppressed heavy-atom graph:

Balaban J is given by the following definition. Consider the hydrogen-suppressed heavy-atom graph:
Build the topological distance matrix (D) on the undirected graph (G=(V,E)). Distances are shortest path lengths in edges; bond orders, charges, isotopes ignored.
For each atom i in V, compute its distance sum (S_i=\sum_{j\in V} D_{ij}).
Let (n=|V|), (m=|E|), and (c) be the number of connected components. Cyclomatic number (\mu=m-n+c).
Balaban index:
J(G)=\frac{m}{\mu+1}\sum_{(i,j)\in E}\frac{1}{\sqrt{S_i S_j}}.
When you are done, return your final answer in the format: solution = <answer to final subproblem>.

Balaban J is given by the following definition. Consider the hydrogen-suppressed heavy-atom graph:
Build the topological distance matrix (D) on the undirected graph (G=(V,E)). Distances are shortest path lengths in edges; bond orders, charges, isotopes ignored.

Let (n=|V|), (m=|E|), and (c) be the number of connected components. Cyclomatic number (\mu=m-n+c).
Balaban index:
J(G)=\frac{m}{\mu+1}\sum_{(i,j)\in E}\frac{1}{\sqrt{S_i S_j}}.

Balaban J is given by the following definition. Consider the hydrogen-suppressed heavy-atom graph:
